source_metadata.title_raw: Benjamin J. Cantu, Jr. - I-18-277 - January 10, 2019 - Manteca

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 10, 2019

Benjamin J. Cantu, Jr.
Planning Principle
829 Mellon Avenue
Manteca, CA 95337

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. 1-18-277

Dear Mr. Cantu, Jr.:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Because your question does not involve a specific pending decision, we are treating this as a request for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

To what extent may you conduct your business and client project undertakings in the City of Manteca before rising to the level of conflict of interest and/or requiring that you recuse yourself during the formal entitlement process of a project?

CONCLUSION

The Act does not prohibit you from continuing to operate your land use and design consulting business while also serving as Mayor of Manteca. However, the Act does prohibit public officials at any level of state or local government from making, participating in making, or in any way attempting to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. Accordingly, it is likely that you will be prohibited from contacting or appearing before City of Manteca

1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. I-18-277
Page No. 2

staff or officials in representation of your clients and will be required to recuse yourself
from any decisions involving your clients' projects.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You were recently elected as the Mayor of Manteca. You have also been in the land use and
design consulting business since 2006. You are a land use planner with over forty years of
experience in community development department management, land use planning, site designing,
land divisions, CEQA, building design, building codes, zoning codes, city codes and standards and
policies. Your basic service consists of assisting clients with the design of projects on their
properties and the management of their projects through the entitlement process. You have
conducted many projects over the years in various jurisdictions, including counties and cities. The
projects have been submitted to community development and building departments, and appeared
for entitlement review and action before agency staff, planning commissions, city councils, and
districts. Working with engineers, landscape architects, land surveyors, and other sub-consultants,
you have prepared formal project plans and documentation for formal submittals to agencies.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making, participating in making, or using his
or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on one or more of the official's interests. Section 87103 also identifies the financial interests
that may give rise to a conflict of interest. Interests from which a conflict of interest may arise are
defined in Section 87103, as pertinent to the facts provided potentially disqualifying interest and
include the following:

• A public official's own finances, or those of a member of his or her immediate family.
(Section 87103.)
• Any business entity in which the public official has a direct or indirect investment worth two
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. (Section 87103(a).)
• Any real property in which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two
thousand dollars ($2,000) or more. (Section 87103(b).)
• Any source of income aggregating five hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided or
promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made. (Section 87103(c).)
• Any business entity in which the public official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)
• Any donor of a gift or gifts aggregating to $500 or more in value received by, or provided
to, or promised to the official within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(e).)

The Commission has defined "making," "participating in making," and "attempting to use
an official position to influence" a decision in Regulation 18704. A public official "makes a
governmental decision" when the official, "officially authorizes or directs any action, votes, appoints
a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any

File No. I-18-277
Page No. 3

contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency." (Section 87100; Regulation 18704(a).) A
public official "participates in a governmental decision if the official provides information, an
opinion, or a recommendation for the purpose of affecting the decision without significant
intervening substantive review." (Section 87100; Regulation 18704(b).)

There are two rules that address whether a public official is using or attempting to use his or
her official position to influence a governmental decision. The first rule applies when the
governmental decision is within or before the public official's own agency or "an agency subject to
the authority or budgetary control of his or her agency for the purpose of affecting a decision." In
these cases, if the official "contacts or appears before" or otherwise attempts to influence, any
member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency, then he or she is attempting to influence a
governmental decision. (Regulation 18704(c)(1).)

The second rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before an agency other
than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control
of the public official's agency. (Regulation 18704(c)(2).) Under this rule, the official cannot act or
purport "to act within his or her authority or on behalf of his or her agency in making the contact"
to influence a decision that will have a material financial effect on his or her economic interests.

For your general assistance, nothing in the Act prohibits you from continuing to operate
your land use and design consulting business while also serving as Mayor of Manteca. However,
you will be prohibited from making, participating, or using your position to influence any decision
with a foreseeable and material effect on your business or any client from which you have received
or been promised income of more than $500 in the prior 12 months. To assist you with determining
when you are disqualified in any particular decision, you may want to consult your agency's
counsel. In addition, you can consult our website for further information concerning conflicts of
interest under the Act, including the publication titled "Recognizing Conflicts of Interest" available
at: http://www.fppc.ca.gov/content/dam/fppc/NS-
Documents/LegalDiv/Conflicts%20of%20Interest/Conflicts-Guide-August-2015-Jan-2016-
Edits.pdf.

More specifically, it is not feasible to advise whether to recuse yourself from a
governmental decision without specific facts regarding a particular governmental decision. We
caution however that you will likely be prohibited from contacting or appearing before City of
Manteca staff or officials in representation of your clients' projects, including submitting
documents under your name on your client's behalf. Additionally, you will likely be required to
recuse yourself from any city council decision involving your clients' projects.

File No. I-18-277
Page No. 4

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Kevin Cornwall
Counsel, Legal Division

KMC:jgl
